item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image could not be sourced reliably and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would assume no.
But the thought of an anthropomorphic male platypus martial arts expert with venom spurs capable of causing excruciating pain immune to analgesics is horrifying in its own way.

item.title: presumable typo fix
item.content:encoded: In the last collapsible it says that 'no unremarkable equipment was found'. Considering that no equipment was then remarked on, I assume that was meant to be 'no remarkable equipment'. I made the edit, please revert if I overstepped or misinterpreted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The 'kill all humans part did seem a little tacked on but apart from that this was a well done skip +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The reason some people don't enjoy this is because they feel it ripped off Planet Of The Apes or they just find it too silly, not like I don't mind, I enjoy this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be honest, I'm kind of baffled by why so many people dislike this, it's really really good. I guess that's why I probably shouldn't stick to 100+ articles like I tend to when browsing SCP casually, you can miss out on many hidden gems like this one. Thanks "Random SCP" button.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will pay good money if they teach this to a Corgi. A fluffy Corgi. Then watch several grown men have the sheit beaten out of them… with Eye of the Tiger in the background.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Teach it to an elephant. Don't ask just do it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hate it but I love it but I hate it but I love it but I hate it.

1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really hate this. Kung Fu Panda meets Planet of the Apes meets my downvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found this pretty entertaining, but it going the Planet of the Apes way seemed rather pointless. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, there must be some massive culture clash here, but I completely love this.

I grew up with Chinese wuxia literature - mainly some Jin Yong, with a dash of Justice Bao and Journey to the West as well - and the idea that, after thousands of years of humans looking at the animals for inspirations for Praying Mantis kung fu or Monkey Stealing Peach kung fu or whatever, now the animals are doing the same thing back to us?

The symmetry there is just delicious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was gonna upvote, because this would allow the Foundation to make Kung Fu Panda and Splinter from TMNT a reality.
But then I saw that this is a rip-off of Planet of the Apes.
neutral

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was waiting to be impressed despite the one-note nature of the concept. Then

dog named Caesar

dohhhh ho ho liek Planet of the Apes Caesar. Followed by

I am Caesar kill all humans

now that was just a straight ripoff of Planet of the Apes Caesar with absolutely no subtlty or original spin

And considering here it's a beloved house pet dog and not a systematically abused/imprisoned chimpanzee, this makes absolutely no sense that the dog would go "KILL ALL HUMANS" out of nowhere, especially with the guy who trained and embiggened its brain.

This is really, really weak. I can't get over the hackneyed Planet of the Apes-ripoff

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually liked it. Yes it brings up a lot of cartoon references to mind, but more in a "this is your cartoon on SCPs" way than a "hey lookee I made a SCP based on xxx". The difference is subtle but basically I find this SCP interesting and creepy enough to stand on its own.
Minor complaint, I would have liked Caesar to be a bit less hostile toward a master who was apparently not mistreating him. That the animals in Foundation custody would like to escape is logical, but that any random pet becomes a rabid animal freedom fighter is a bit too much for my tastes (oh, and Caesar rings a bit too much of Planet of The Apes).

Also, does the image come from a diagram about the Enpi kata in karate ?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: okay. now a new question: would they try to mix their anomalies into the fighting style?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, the dog was gunned down. As far as animal SCPs are concerned, placental mammal SCPs would start imitating the martial art and their appearance and behavior would change the way other animals change, but their anomalies would likely persist. Other SCPs would go unaffected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the dog's still on the loose, right? because if so, he's most likely teaching other animals, raising an army, planing an attack…

one of the weirdest villains the world has ever seen. we are surely doomed by his paws. +1 the warrior pup. let's hope he never meets Scrappy, or both sides of the animal kingdom civil war are doomed.

question: what would happen if SCP-953 saw anyone performing this? or any animal SCP, for that matter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm, I wanted to like this, despite the cartoon animal hints (why else the size normalising?) but the malevolence and ability to talk just completely destroyed it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It says that only placental mammals are affected, but not marsupials. What about monotremes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember liking this a lot when I first read it, but on a re-read, I think the experiment logs and final few addenda carry a good if slightly silly concept a little too far in the direction of the generically threatening. I think I'd prefer it if it didn't make the animals talk.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bast this is brilliant.
Worst of all, the dog didn't appear mistreated or anything by its master, but still attempted to "rise up" - this thing seems to have been devised with malicious intent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Until Caeser started ranting, I'd figured that the animals were turning out homicidal because every single test involved them being killed one by one.

item.title: Re: Creator post
item.content:encoded: This is moderately successful, you don't really need to revise it.

item.title: Creator post
item.content:encoded:
Yowza! So this scip is clearly … divisive? About half of you seem to love it, and half of you seem to hate it. Better to be controversial than boring, I suppose, but the reactions I'm seeing are a little more extreme than I was expecting.

A couple quick notes for those who are wondering:
1) No, I wasn't inspired by Kung Fu Panda, Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles, or anything similar. If you must know where this came from, here's the link. (SFW)
2) No, I'm not a furry. Not that it matters.
3) Yes, this scip is a little silly. I wanted to see if I could take an inherently slightly bizarre premise and write it up completely straight-faced, and I think I did that part okay. I think it's just that the premise is too bizarre for a lot of readers.

All this being said, I'm working on a new version of this scip in my sandbox. It's still a martial art, and it still has some connection to animals, but it goes in a completely different direction. I would really appreciate reactions to that version—is it better than this? More boring? Too cliched? Should I replace this scip with the new version? If you have any comments, please go to the original forum thread for this scip and let me know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Um
WTF IS THIS

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe the homicidal response is more physical in nature. Purebred Dobermans (and certain other breeds) have a problem where the skull stops growing before the brain does, causing massive headaches. In extreme cases, the dog goes 'mad' and needs to be put down. This became a huge issue in the early 80's when Dobies were very popular as guard/attack dogs. The attack reflex, combined with the mind-shattering migraines they suffered, caused a lack of IFF that left many owners injured.

Add to that that most animal skulls, even if scaled up to human size, do not have a brainpan large enough to fit a human brain. I would think putting a human-sized brain in a dog would require something resembling the Pluto costume for a head (on a 1m tall body). Development of language centers, more complex balance-control and other improvements would require a sizeable increase in grey matter. Even if the area expanded as the animal evolved, most likely the brain would grow at a much faster rate, causing the above problem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe, just maybe, this is where the idea of furries come from. Someone discovered these arts centuries ago and, even though most was lost, the idea remained. Possibly these altered animals are violent because we don't have the full set of movements. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kung fu panda was the very first thing I thought.

And then it took that ball and ran with it. Kung fu panda is not significantly creepier/scarier off he kills people. He's a bear, he can do that anyway. So the idea of any animal becoming anthroporphic and learning a martial art is too cartoonish to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dude, i tought it when i realized what this SCP was.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this, for some reason. It just comes off as so creepy to have walking, talking, ripped animals that hate people. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like that it doesn't work on marsupials. Despite the subject matter, it all feels very authentic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This would make a good episode of Doctor Who.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Care to explain your reasoning there? It's a mashup of two things that we see constantly around here. I'm tired of it. Please to inform me why that's "stupid".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved it! Especially the YouTube videos leading to the discovery of the SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly. I felt the same way, and you summed it up better than I did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is definitely not bad. Maybe a bit clichéd, but definitely not bad. Never seen a martial arts SCP before, and also, nice use of the picture. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was rolling with the SCP and enjoying it quite a lot, right up until the dog started talking, where it lost my support. It's like there's a line in the article with a note saying "AND HERE COMES THE CREEPY PART". Personally I'd enjoy it just fine if the SCP made the animals turn into bipedal kung fu things (whether or not they talk… I'd go with "no"), and they react in various ways. Some become peaceful monk-like practitioners, others bullies, others militant or whatever. The fact that it turns all of them into psychopaths that want to kung fu the hell out of humans for the sake of kung fuing the hell out of humans is just…. boring to me, I guess.

No vote for now.

item.title: Mod Post
item.content:encoded: No personal attacks, Pata H. This is an official warning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We're also stocked up on stupid downvote reasons yet you seem to have no problem shoveling them onto the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There may be room for improvement but I like the overall idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, these two are just frustrating to me because they're things I really love in real life executed poorly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Talking animals what fight good. Sorry, we're all stocked up here. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well. I wouldn't really call it "silly" so much as "odd" or "bizarre". Silly to me is something like Jester where it's impossible to take seriously because of how corny and cliched it is. 1258 doesn't give that feel to me. To me, this is something that, while it does have the potential to spawn silly images, still can be taken seriously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kinda like how this takes a pretty ridiculous concept and executes it in a very dry, clinical fashion. I'm not quite ready to upvote, but we'll see what happens. Also, the whole time I was reading it, I kept thinking, "What if this is Kain's true backstory?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Perhaps I should have said "No references or jokes that I get".
I've seen PotA, but I can't say I recall much more than the general plot and Heston's two big lines.

Caesar, and variations on it, just seems like a common dog name, to me. I blame it on the dog food brand.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not too sure on the no references or jokes. Naming the dog Caesar could be a reference to the Planet of the Apes series, in which the first ape to really stand up to mankind was named Caesar, and he went on to lead the ape rebellion and became a religious figure to the apes of the future.

Could be a coincidence though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I realized the base concept, I hated it. Splinter as an SCP is just silly.
But the absolute dead-pan clinical tone, no references or jokes, gradually brought me up to neutral.
The YouTube montage edged me up to positive, because while it is a bit funny, it's 100% realistic. This is what I would do in the situation. Or maybe turn it into an act and go on Leno.
The escalation hit me totally out of left field, and was all the more effective for being unsuspected. Good job with a difficult concept. +1

I can't help imagining the animal-fu warriors channeling Joe Pesci. "Does my kung-fu amuse you?! I'm just a silly animal, put here to entertain you with my antics, lookit the doggie, he thinks he's a widdle warrior, IS THAT WHAT YOU THINK?! Well, what do you think of this!" #flying dragondoberman face kick#

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How is being a dog a disadvantage?
A dog pumped up to the size of a man is going to be signifigantly stronger than a human, just de facto. In the same way that a gorilla is human-scale but could tear your arm off without even meaning to.

Think of what an ordinary german sheperd attack dog can do to an intruder, then multiply strength and reach by three, then factor in the intelligence to use advanced planning and tactics, ambushes, hit and run, booby-traps, etc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, the "creep" is an Imperial unit equivalent to twenty drams of ominosity applied to the fear glands. Thank god for decimalization.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I rather like the idea that, like the Sievert, the Slender has to be millidivided to have any sensible use, because someone experiencing 1 Slender of creepiness would be driven into a coma with fear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is worrying like a Furry Creator to me. And worse than that, a Kung-Fu FUrry Creator. I can't downvote it because it's not badly written and the idea is clever, but that alone prevents an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. The writing on the addendum where Caesar goes full postal isn't terribly good, and all of the phrasing in the talking-animal sections is kind of "standard creepy broken English" to me. But it's pretty goddamn unique, and it gave me exactly six and a half creeps.1 Take this upvote.

Footnotes
1. Or in metric, 23.2 millislenders.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually liked that bit. It makes sense that the Foundation would have people browsing the media for anomalous activity. Granted, it would turn up 99 false positives for every actual anomaly, but it still seems worth it. They can't always rely on having operatives in just the right place at just the right time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this article a lot, up until the dog started murdering people. It seemed forced, like the writer was trying to tack on extra danger just to make the SCP more threatening. I also don't understand how the dog was able to take down a group of armed soldiers. Even the world's best martial artist couldn't do that, and he wouldn't have the massive disadvantage of being a dog. The only way it'd make sense is if the SCP also gave animals superhuman strength and speed, but that'd be kinda dumb and it wasn't specified.

No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It didn't bother me at first, but it occurs to me that the idea of the Foundation looking for SCPs via Google is just plain silly.

"Sir! I've got something big here! All these people have been reporting sightings of something called 'the slender man'…"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not a very good idea, and why the hell would you redact the images? Just silly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hamato Yoshi's martial art was this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Has my scip really become your benchmark of bad? ;_;
Edit: And to add, I actually really like this, and I see exactly why they'd redact what was in the picture. I thought it was nice and chilling while being pretty funny.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Guys, I think we have a containment breach on our hands.
